text.title: Enforcement Penalties and Exemptions
text.summary: Enforcement consequences by jurisdiction and valid exemptions — with documentation requirements for each exemption claim.

example.code: Consequence | Typical Range Private lawsuit settlement | $5,000 - $75,000+ Legal fees (plaintiff) | $50,000 - $200,000+ Consent decree + monitoring | Multi-year compliance program DOJ investigation | Reputational + remediation cost Loss of federal funding | For government entities
example.note: Thousands of ADA web lawsuits filed annually. Serial plaintiffs target e-commerce, banking, and healthcare.

example.code: Consequence | Notes Fine up to 4% of annual revenue | Per member state enforcement Product withdrawal from market | If non-conformant after deadline Prohibition of services | Regulator order Compensation to affected individuals | Civil action

list.item: Canada: Complaints to CRTC or provincial accessibility commissioners.
UK: Unlawful discrimination claims under Equality Act 2010.
Australia: Complaints to Australian Human Rights Commission.

rule.statement: Exemptions are narrow, must be individually documented, and always require an accessible alternative to be provided.
rule.rationale: Regulators and courts reject blanket exemptions. Each exemption needs a written justification, scope limitation, and alternative access path.

list.item: Pre-existing content — content published before the compliance deadline and not subsequently altered.
Archived content — clearly marked as archived, not updated, not relied on for active services.
Third-party content not under organizational control — must still provide an accessible alternative.
Undue burden — must document why compliance is an undue financial or administrative burden AND provide an alternative.
Fundamental alteration — compliance would change the fundamental nature of the service; must be documented and alternative provided.

antipattern.description: Claiming "undue burden" without a documented cost analysis and alternative access plan.
antipattern.reason: Courts and regulators reject undocumented exemptions; the burden of proof is on the organization claiming the exemption.
